votes.reason: The Python code iteratively implements the exact same string splitting logic as the Coq recursive function, correctly handling separators and the accumulator.
The Python code correctly implements the string splitting logic defined in the Coq functions, handling separators and accumulating words equivalently.
The Python code correctly implements the string splitting logic iteratively, matching the recursive behavior of the Coq function on all concrete inputs.

The Python code correctly implements the same logic as the Coq code. The is_separator function checks for space or comma. The words_string_aux function iteratively builds words by accumulating non-separator characters and appending completed words when separators are encountered, matching the Coq recursive definition. The words_string_spec function returns whether the result equals words_string_aux(s, ""), which corresponds to the Coq Prop equality. Both implementations will produce the same list of words for any concrete input string.

The Python code correctly implements the same logic as the Coq code. The is_separator function checks for space or comma. The words_string_aux function iterates through characters, accumulating non-separator characters and splitting on separators, which matches the Coq recursive definition. The spec function compares the result to words_string_aux(s, "") just like the Coq version. The iterative Python implementation produces the same results as the recursive Coq implementation for all concrete string inputs.

The Python code correctly implements the same logic as the Coq code. The is_separator function checks for space or comma. The words_string_aux function iteratively builds words by accumulating non-separator characters and appending completed words when separators are encountered, matching the Coq recursive definition. The words_string_spec function returns whether the result equals words_string_aux(s, ""), which matches the Coq specification. The iterative Python implementation produces the same results as the recursive Coq implementation for all concrete string inputs.

Both implementations split the input into non-empty tokens separated by space or comma, skip consecutive separators, and include the final token if present. The Python loop mirrors the Coq recursion on all concrete inputs.
Python mirrors the Coq logic: same separators (space/comma), same accumulation and emission of tokens on separators and at end, correctly handling empty accumulators and consecutive/edge separators.

Python mirrors the Coq logic: it treats space/comma as separators, builds tokens via an accumulator, emits a token on separators only when non-empty, and appends any final token. words_string_spec checks equality with words_string_aux(s, ""), matching the Coq spec.
